speaker: interviewer
text: And now we're going to the next factory. OK. Anna, you were telling me about the second factory now in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Was in Moravia, in [NON-ENGLISH]. And he has a house there. And Mrs. Schindler, she live in this house. Sometime she was in the window and watching us when we've been outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet Mrs. Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- not-- just after war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I mean, in the museum, I met her. But I saw her from the window. This time she was a young lady. Now she's old. Very kind, very nice lady. She did have some wool. I don't know where she took the wool and to give to us, so we been knitting sweaters for us because we've been not busy. So this was a good-- good for us to knit. And was winter, so was good to have the sweaters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna, I want to ask you, you said you weren't busy. Can you tell me what a typical day was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we been busy, or we wasn't busy?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you said you weren't busy. Can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --about a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We no--

speaker: interviewer
text: You woke up, what time? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean not busy?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not busy because we didn't work a lot in this factory. So we woke up early, like 5 o'clock, went-- went to the appell. And then walk to the factory. And then it depends. When I work for the Emalia, so we-- we-- we putted this-- I told you this--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the first factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first factory. But now you ask about the Czechoslovakia factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so they been big machines. And I-- nobody trained me. I didn't know how to operate the machines. Was not the enamel work anymore, just the arms work. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of arms? What were you making?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They say that the V-- this was the-- the newest-- that we work on the-- the V arms. It was the newest. What-- was very powerful. The German find out. But then we-- we been hearing that it not work, nothing what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they guns? What were they, bullets? What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just say arms and V. The very modern somethings. Somebody find out. So this is what he got to do in this factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just sit by a tall machine and was like a-- a tube. And you put around metal there and-- and push with your legs, and then down. Very boring work. But was not too much. Then we didn't do nothing. We've been sitting around. we were just talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when Schindler would come in, what would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we have the sign. And we make six. If somebody noticed him or another person, some German or woman German, so they make the sign six. And everybody went to his place. We didn't talk. We pretend we work hard. And that this what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you weren't working, what were you talking about with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just we've been cooking, [LAUGHS] just about food. Mostly woman been talking and about hoping that one time the war will finish, and we will be with our families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, always even, for example, where you sleep. So in the room is lots of girls. And we make friends. We've been friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you help each other to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just be optimistic. We been and talking about end the war, talking about good food, what we will cook when we come back. So somebody say, potatoes and sour milk. This-- [LAUGHS] somebody like meat, we talk how we cook and what we will eat when we come back home-- stuff like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any cultural activities? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the girl who was the poet, she-- she write the poems. And she-- she talk about this. And she was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would she read them aloud?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the poems about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About surviving, about nature, about people. Were very good poems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other activities? Any singing, dancing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, was not other activities-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about religious observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends. You have very religious people when it was Yom Kippur, so they've been fasting.

speaker: interviewer
text: They fasted?

speaker: interviewee
text: They've been fasting, yeah. Shabbat, you couldn't keep Shabbat because you have to go to the factories. So Saturday, you have to work. When it was nice sun, so we went outside to the-- on sun. But this was only at Schindler, not-- not in Plaszow. We were not going, sit in the-- take sunshine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you in the factory with Jews whom you had known from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all new.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- yeah, but very quick, we make friends. So this was not problem. Out

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of backgrounds did they come from? What had they done for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, they've been in school. And in concentration camp, they interrupt school. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any famous persons in there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But was there one doctor. One surgeon was there. And one beautiful girl-- she was so sick. She got diarhhea, and she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get her medical attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give her, but-- but she didn't survive. Beautiful girl. She was so beautiful that the German said he will make for her special place a bury-- to bury her.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, the one German who was in charge. He didn't put her with the grave where, you know, the mass grave. Just, he make a special place for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about her? Where she was from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. Just I know she got diarhhea. And it was not-- maybe they will cure her now, you know. But-- but I was also sick. I was-- I have also diarrhea. But we have a doctor. His name was Dr. Rubenstein. And [? Sulina ?] was his nurse. He too had been for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they prisoners with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they've been prisoners, too. And I was really very sick and went to him. And they didn't give me any medicine. Just he put me on diet eat apples. And they got apples for me. And this cure me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you sick for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Few days. And then I was OK again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were sick, did they allow you to rest? Or did you have to go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: They allow me to rest, yeah. This was under Schindler, so-- so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else ever get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I couldn't go to work, I was so weak. I-- I have to be in bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other prisoners--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they get sick. We-- this why we have the doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one doctor, and he has a nurse. [? Sulina ?] was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any items of religious observance--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Jews smuggled in with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but two brothers been there, very religious guys. And them been talking about holy days and were praying. They probably pray, but I doubt that they have some prayers books or something. Tallis, I doubt that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They probably didn't have any tallis because they went through Bergen-Belsen. This was a big camp. And they have to take off the-- the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Oskar Schindler during this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've been in his camp in Moravia. Still, he was always great guy. He was always very nice to us, and good looking, and very polite, kind. We just love him. Everybody loves him a lot. Always good word to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- and he wasn't scared, you know this? What I wondered that somebody can tell on him and take away the factory from him. But nobody was against him He just have friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he tried to have the-- the Nazis on his side. So he bribed them and party with them. And-- and he could arrange everything what he wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start to see any changes in him toward the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: For better or for worse?

speaker: interviewer
text: Either.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was always all the same. And when the Russian came, I saw him in there from the yard and surrounded with the Jewish elderly. And he was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Surrounded with whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish elderly surround him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish elderly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elderly people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seniors. And he was trembling. He was shaking. He was so scared. But they took him to Palestine. And they-- he-- they say, you save us. Now w we save your life. And they ask the Russian commandant, they-- they explain that he wasn't a regular Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go--

speaker: interviewee
text: He has the swastika still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to that time for more detail. Tell me what happened. The Russians came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the commandant, the-- was a Jewish guy. And in the-- in the Jewish language he say, [YIDDISH]. You not-- will go back to-- to Poland. He's big anti-Semitist. Just go to-- to Israel. This was Palestine this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: When are we talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What time are we talking about now when the Russian-Jewish commander came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just when the liberation started. So the-- but what was interesting, that the German been digging a hole for us, a big hole, the Nazi. No Schindler. Just the Nazi. They knew is the Jewish camp there. So they've been digging a big hole to put us there and to shoot us. And the-- and this was in Czechoslovakia. So the Czech partisan came. And they find out this. And they start-- start to hear, the Russian is coming. And this was not true. They came later, around maybe two, three days later. That they knew they are going to-- to liberate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: The partisans-- the Czech partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they've been hearing, Russian are coming. Russian are coming. And you know the German, they-- they've been good in killing the-- the [INAUDIBLE] people who didn't have arms. But they-- how you call these people who scared? How you call-- what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cowards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cowards. They been big coward, so they start to run away. And so they save our lives. Otherwise, they-- they will shoot us, so the Czech partisans, they save our lives. They knew about the hole what they are digging for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, when did the Russians arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We needed lots of miracles, you know, and this was one of the miracles, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your other miracles?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that they didn't take me to-- to the gas chamber was a miracle. That I went-- that I been with Schindler, and he protect us-- this was a miracle. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians arrived, where was Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the back yard and-- and-- and scared and tremble. So-- so they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why he was scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was a Nazi. He has a swastika in his-- on his garment. And the-- the-- the Jewish, elderly explain to them. Stop? Then he was a good Nazi, that he save us all. So they-- they didn't kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when they said this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember them saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this what they saying. He was a good-- he was good man. He not deserve to be killed because he save all these people here. And they understand, and they didn't touch him no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom did they tell this to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Jewish commander--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- yeah, he was a Jewish commander. And he talk in Yiddish to us. [YIDDISH] You not go-- I don't know Yiddish to say. But you-- you don't go back to Poland. It's not good for you. Just go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next was freedom. Then we went home. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we look home. This was end-- this was end of the war, what-- what I would describe now. And everybody went however they could-- a little by train, a little walk, a little cars. And soldiers took us. And took maybe one-- one week to get home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to take with you? How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just in-- I didn't have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: These shoes-- what you see here, what I have the-- the high shoes are-- was ripped. Always, I have the same shoes. And always we've been scared that you will not lose these shoes. What to do if you don't have shoes, and only the one shoe? They've been ripped completely. If the war is longer, I would be barefoot. There was not a store that you can buy shoes. You didn't have money even if were-- if there would be a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- everybody kept the shoes. And were very important was also toothbrush. Not lose the toothbrush. If you lose, what you will do? You cannot buy another toothbrush. That were, like, small thing, you know? But-- but important details, but important. Whom did you travel with?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went back? Well, it's a group of boys and girls and. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the-- how we walk back home, or-- or travel, the Czech people give us food. They've been standing with food, bread, and tried to help. They've been nice. They knew we'd been prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I go to Zakopane and To see where there is somebody back. Was nobody back. I went back to Cracow. And there, I've been going every day and searching, searching, asking people and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you searching for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For my family, whether they came back. Looking for them, and unfortunately, no-- nobody. But they told me that my cousin came back from Canada. Herman is his name. And they told me in what city. And I was there also looking for family. So we met together. And he say he look for family. I say, I look for family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had he been during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Palestine, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Canada, he is now. He-- this his home in Canada, in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: But during the war, he was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- during the war, he was in English [INAUDIBLE]-- and on motorcycle they went-- on motorcycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But English-- from English soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you hooked up with your cousin. And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, then he wents-- he went to Canada. And I stay in Cracow. But he-- first, he arrange for me a sanatorium after war, that I recover. And there I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- what sanatorium were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was in the High Tatra in the mountains for tuberculose. But they clean up this and for us for the-- for the survivors. They make a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was UNRRA, a American organization. And they brought food and clothes-- lots of clothes we can choose, shoes. And food for three weeks, it was arranged that you-- you are, for free, can eat and have a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks. And there I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody told me that here is a young lawyer, and he would like to meet me. What did I want? I say, OK. And so he started to talk to me. And I-- I was from Poland, and I-- he's from Czechoslovakia. And I didn't know this language, Slovak, or Czech. But he knew German, and I knew German. So we communicate in German. And he says, I have nobody, only old mother and a brother. If you have nowhere to go, you welcome come to my house. So I went to Poland, liquidate what I have there, take with me the little stuff, and came to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything left in your family home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just a little stuff, what I have with me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to your home, what did you find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing I found in this home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just stuff what after-- after the liberation they gave us clothes and small stuff. This I have with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: --had you husband been during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Hungarian, in Hungaria on forget papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: On forged papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forged papers, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he living?

speaker: interviewee
text: How he was living there? In fear. Very hard. Very hard. And he has his mother there and brother and take care of them. He was the youngest. And they walked through the mountains to get there. He hire-- they hired somebody who knew the way. And they pay for it. And-- and they'd been hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he earn money?

speaker: interviewee
text: How they earn money?

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd he earn money to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did have some money from before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about you went back. You got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For-- for a while, I stayed in the house. And we knew each other better. And then he proposed to me, and we married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Martin, it called, in Slovakia. Then I didn't go back to Poland no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated in Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. Very good. I-- I didn't have any problem. I learn first the language because it's-- it's similar to Polish, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there'd been. It was a community. And he open his store with his father, was with liquor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Liquor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So he opened this. And then we have a good living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any thoughts of going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did have. And we want to go. But Sonia, my daughter, she-- I was pregnant and have a baby. And then come news that it's very hard life in there with a little baby. That is too risky to go. So then we didn't go. Then when the kids been bigger, then we'd immigrate to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you immigrate to Israel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, back in Slovakia, what was your religious observance like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean when I was married? My husband, he wasn't very religious. And I-- I have problems after war to believe in-- in some-- in God or believe something, you know? It was very hard for me. But then I realized that be like a wild animal and not believe in nothing is worse than believe. So I-- I started to be a believer. I started to go to synagogue to pray, and still I am in Temple Emanuel. Belong to Temple Emanuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you say your experience during the war affected your religious beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I felt that I-- I-- I was in dilemma. I didn't know what to do. First I say, cannot be God if he could do this to me. But then [INAUDIBLE] God was also not good. So-- so I stay with my religion. I was born Jewish, and I-- I still Jewish. I practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you went to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: You saw me took the candle. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you when you arrived in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will be arrive in Israel? Well, very hard. Very hard because come from communist country, you need some money to start. And didn't let us take any money. So it was very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you work in Israel, you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: We find work. We have-- there's, in Be'er Sheva. They put us in Be'er Sheva. And I find out that somebody from [NON-ENGLISH], from Poland, is there. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. And he has a-- a hotel and coffeehouse. And he give me work to do. Was hard for me, but-- but I have a job. And then my husband find a job. And the kids went to kibbutz, all three. So-- so we start. But was not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live on a kibbutz also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We've been in Be'er Sheva, and the kids went to kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years. And here in this house live my brother-- live my husband brother. And he wrote us letters that he's very sick and whether I can come and help him. I didn't know what to do. But family decided that yeah, that I have to go because they-- he was my husband. Stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we can continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Continue. He want my husband here to come, his brother. But Paul say, what-- what's father can help? He wasn't-- he didn't know how to cook and to clean and take care of a sick person. They decided that I will be the-- the person who can-- he can use me. So I came here by myself. All my family, I leave there. And I have [INAUDIBLE]-- you know [INAUDIBLE], what it is, a Zionistic Jewish organization-- I have a job there. So I leave the job, my family behind, and came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your family follow?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came maybe-- I came-- I think in one year, they came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all three, they came-- Julius, my son, Paul, and my husband. Sonia, she was married, so she-- she stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just stop there.
